XGetDevices

The XGetDevices function queries the peripheral connectivity state for a specified device type. 

DWORD XGetDevices(
  PXPP_DEVICE_TYPE DeviceType
);

Parameters

DeviceType
[in] Specifies the device type to query. DeviceType must be of one the following values:
ValueDescription
XDEVICE_TYPE_GAMEPADAn Xbox Controller.
XDEVICE_TYPE_MEMORY_UNITAn Xbox Memory Unit.
XDEVICE_TYPE_VOICE_MICROPHONEXbox Communicator microphone.
XDEVICE_TYPE_VOICE_HEADPHONEXbox Communicator headphone.
XDEVICE_TYPE_DEBUG_KEYBOARDA debug keyboard. Do not use in shipping titles. See Remarks below.
XDEVICE_TYPE_DEBUG_MOUSEA debug mouse. Do not use in shipping titles. See Remarks below.

Note   All game controllers (wheels, joysticks, lightguns, and game pads, for example) use XDEVICE_TYPE_GAMEPAD.

Return Values

If the function succeeds, the return value is a DWORD bitmask that indicates the port or slot locations of connected devices of the specified device type.

The return value is a bitwise OR of masks representing each of the ports (or slots) to which a device of the specified type is connected. The tables below provide the names and values of the masks. The values are provided so that code may check the bits in a loop without using the mask names. Depending on whether the device plugs directly into an Xbox port (controllers, for example), or into a slot on a controller (memory units, for example), the returned bits are interpreted differently.

Direct Port Connection Devices
Mask Name:
Value:
XDEVICE_PORT0_MASK (1<<0)
XDEVICE_PORT1_MASK (1<<1)
XDEVICE_PORT2_MASK (1<<2)
XDEVICE_PORT3_MASK (1<<3)

Slot Connection Devices
Mask Name:
Value:
XDEVICE_PORT0_TOP_MASK (1<<0)
XDEVICE_PORT1_TOP_MASK (1<<1)
XDEVICE_PORT2_TOP_MASK (1<<2)
XDEVICE_PORT3_TOP_MASK (1<<3)
XDEVICE_PORT0_BOTTOM_MASK (1<<16)
XDEVICE_PORT1_BOTTOM_MASK (1<<17)
XDEVICE_PORT2_BOTTOM_MASK (1<<18)
XDEVICE_PORT3_BOTTOM_MASK (1<<19)

If no devices were found, the return value is 0.

Remarks

Before calling XGetDevices, a title must call XInitDevices to initialize the Xbox peripherals.

The XGetDevices function gets the complete peripheral connectivity state for a given device type. The function returns a bitmap of the devices of that type that are currently connected. The interpretation of the bitmap depends on whether the devices of the requested type plug directly into the console or if they plug into an Xbox controller expansion slot.

In general, titles call this function to receive a snapshot of the system and then call XGetDeviceChanges periodically to get dynamic insertions and removals.

Device enumeration is an asynchronous process. Users may insert or remove devices at any time. Enumeration is done in the background in response to interrupts. While it uses few CPU cycles, in some cases, enumeration takes approximately a second to complete. The peripheral drivers are part of the XTL and are initialized late in the boot cycle. Therefore, it is common that the first call to XGetDevices reports only a subset of the devices connected at boot. These devices will be reported via XGetDevices or XGetDeviceChanges as they are enumerated. Keep this in mind when writing code for your games.

To use XDEVICE_TYPE_DEBUG_KEYBOARD, #define DEBUG_KEYBOARD before including Xtl.h. Do not use XDEVICE_TYPE_DEBUG_KEYBOARD in shipping retail game titles.

To use XDEVICE_TYPE_DEBUG_MOUSE, #define DEBUG_MOUSE before including Xtl.h. Do not use XDEVICE_TYPE_DEBUG_MOUSE in shipping retail game titles.

Requirements

Header: Declared in Xbox.h, for include use Xtl.h.
Import Library: Use Xapilib.lib

See Also

Input Device Functions, Determining Connected Devices | XInitDevices, XGetDeviceChanges, XInputOpen, XMountMU
